title: NA-ULIP
word_text:
Calculate Mortality Charges in ULIP
Mortality Charge is the bone of contention in ULIPs because many consider it primarily an investment vehicle. However, the fact is that mortality charges are part of any insurance product and it is easy to see why. The insurer provides a life cover which is the sum assured payable to the nominee in case of death of the insured. The mortality charges are for providing the life cover under the policy. It is for this reason that an amount is deducted from the fund value as mortality charge.
Let's understand this with the help of an example. A 35-year old Mr X purchases a ULIP with an annual premium of ` 100,000 and a sum assured of ` 10,00,000. The death benefit payable in this policy is higher of sum assured or fund value. In case of unfortunate death of Mr X in the 4th year of purchasing the policy, the amount receivable by the nominee is higher of the fund value which is assumed to have grown to ` 5.5 lakhs or sum assured of ` 10 lakhs. In this example, the nominee will receive ` 10 lakhs as the death benefit. This risk of providing life cover is borne by the insurer, and mortality charges are deducted towards covering this risk.
How Is Mortality Charge Calculated?
Mortality Charge depends on factors such as age, amount of coverage (sum assured) of the policyholder, gender, smoking habits, health factors, etc. and is deducted on a monthly basis. It is generally lower for younger & healthier individuals. It increases, with the advancement of the policyholder's age.
The mortality rate (as on attained age) is per ` 1,000 of the cover or the sum at risk. It is calculated monthly based on the following formula:
Mortality charge = mortality rate (for the attained age) x sum at risk/1000 x 1/12
When Shall Lower Mortality Charges Apply?
Mortality charges in ULIPs reduce as the policy term progresses. This is because ULIPs usually promise a higher sum assured or the fund value as a death benefit. If the sum assured is higher than the available fund value, the insurance company has to cover the difference between the fund value and the sum assured. This difference is called the sum at risk.
As the policy term advances, the fund value might increase with the returns earned from market-linked securities. As the fund value increases, the sum at risk reduces. As the sum at risk reduces, the mortality charge would also reduce since the insurer’s liability would reduce.
Alternatively, if the fund value falls and the sum at risk increases, the mortality charge would also increase.
So, usually, lower mortality charges apply as the policy term advances.
Moreover, in some plans, you have the option to reduce the sum assured during the policy tenure. If you reduce the sum assured, the sum at risk would also reduce. This would also reduce the mortality charge.
What If You Get Back The Entire Mortality Charge?
Looking at customer's expectation from a ULIP, Bajaj Allianz Life Insurance has pioneered the concept of Return of Mortality Charges (RoMC) with its recently launched products Bajaj Allianz Life Goal Assure & Bajaj Allianz Life Goal Based Savings. It makes ULIPs more investor-friendly and increases the return on maturity.
With this feature, the policyholder gets back all the mortality charge deducted over the course of the policy term at the time of maturity. This amount is added to the Fund Value at maturity. The only factor that the policyholder needs to ensure is that the policy is not surrendered or discontinued and all the premiums have been paid on time.
It is easy to see that mortality charge is an integral part of any insurance product. At the same time, features like RoMC enhance the benefit for the policyholders and help them to reach their life goals faster.
Factors That Affect The Mortality Charges For Unit Linked Insurance Plans:
The mortality charges under ULIPs depend on a lot of factors. These factors are as follows –
Age Of The Insured
Age has a direct impact on mortality risk. The older you are, the higher the risk, and vice versa. Thus, the mortality charge is higher for people who are older compared to those who are young.
Sum Assured
The sum assured is the coverage availed under the plan. It represents the insurer’s liability in the case of a death claim and directly affects the mortality charge. The higher the sum assured, the higher the charge would be and vice versa.
Sum At Risk
The sum at risk is the difference between the sum assured and the fund value. If the sum assured is more than the fund value, the insurance company pays the sum assured as the death benefit. This is equal to the sum at risk along with the fund value. Thus, if the sum at risk is higher, the mortality charge would be higher and vice versa.
Gender
Females are statistically found to have a lower mortality risk compared to males1. As such, the mortality charges for females might be lower.
Lifestyle Habits
Smokers have a higher health and mortality risk compared to non-smokers. Similar is the case with people who consume a lot of alcohol. That is why the mortality charge is higher if your lifestyle habits include smoking and drinking.
Existing Health Conditions
If you have existing medical complications, you pose a higher mortality risk than normal lives. As such, the mortality charge is higher for individuals with existing health conditions.
Type Of ULIP
There are two main types of ULIPs2. One pays higher of the sum assured or the fund value on death, and the other which pays both the sum assured and the fund value as a death benefit. In the latter case, the sum at risk is higher since the insurance company has to pay the sum assured at all times. Thus, the second types of ULIPs have a higher mortality charge than the first type.
FAQs
Are mortality charges levied on all Unit Linked Insurance Plans?
All ULIPs are life insurance plans providing insurance protection against premature demise. Thus, every ULIP has a mortality charge to pay for the insurance coverage provided.
What factors affect the calculation of mortality charges in ULIPs?
Factors used for the calculation of mortality charges in ULIPs include age, gender, sum assured, lifestyle habits, type of ULIP, existing medical conditions, etc.
Will mortality charges be refunded on maturity?
Some ULIPs refund the mortality charges deducted during the policy tenure if the plan matures3. So, if you opt for such plans and survive the chosen policy tenure, you will receive the mortality charges on maturity.
Is the entire amount of Mortality Charges for ULIPs levied upfront?
No, ULIP mortality charges are deducted from the fund value each month by canceling the relevant number of units3. They are not levied upfront.
